contents.title: GameNext Research Bridge: Near-Term Features and Long-Term Speculation

sections.title: Governance, Ratios, and Topic Lifecycle
sections.summary: Defines how near-term and speculative topics coexist, how ratios are enforced, and how topics evolve over time.

designDecisions.statement: Maintain 60–70% of active research topics in `open` or `in-progress` states that are implementable on current PC/console/engine hardware, with the remaining 30–40% reserved for speculative and forward-looking research.
rationale: Prevents the repository from drifting into purely theoretical work.
Ensures continuous delivery of features, optimizations, and integration wins for developers.
Creates a disciplined pipeline where speculative topics must be anchored to concrete sub-topics.
implications: New speculative topics must declare at least one concrete anchor topicId in the same category.
CI or tooling can periodically compute this ratio and warn when speculative load is too high.

designDecisions.statement: All research topics follow a lifecycle with states: `open` → `in-progress` → `implemented` or `blocked` → optionally `retired`.
rationale: Provides a clear, machine-trackable lifecycle for research objects.
Enables dashboards and AI tools to focus on actionable work.
Supports historical analysis of which speculative ideas matured into implemented features.

missingPieces: Automated tooling that computes the near-term vs speculative ratio and enforces category-level thresholds.
Dashboards or reports that highlight aging `open` topics with no linked experiments or commits.
Policies for when and how to transition `speculative` topics to `open` once feasibility is proven.

proposedFunctions.description: Scans all topics and returns the proportion of near-term (`open`, `in-progress`) vs `speculative` topics.

proposedFunctions.signature: enforce_ratio_policy(topics: [ResearchTopic], min_near_term: f32, max_speculative: f32) -> PolicyReport
proposedFunctions.description: Evaluates whether the current set of topics violates configured ratios and produces human-readable and machine-readable warnings.

sections.title: Anchoring Speculative Topics to Concrete Work
sections.summary: Formalizes how long-term, speculative ideas are tied to near-term, implementable sub-topics.

patterns.mechanism: The speculative work on multi-component signalQuality models is anchored by concrete tasks to calibrate scalar qualityGuard behavior on existing engines and displays.

math.scalar_guard: guard = clamp01(signalQuality / t)  (for signalQuality < t),  guard = 1.0  (for signalQuality >= t)
math.multi_component_quality: signalQuality_ext = w1 * spatialCoverage + w2 * SNR + w3 * motionRobustness

proofSketch: Given signalQuality_ext ∈ [0,1] and weights w1, w2, w3 ≥ 0 with w1 + w2 + w3 = 1, signalQuality_ext is bounded in [0,1].
The collapse function signalQuality = clamp01(signalQuality_ext) preserves bounds and monotonicity.
Existing guard formulas and shaders that take a scalar signalQuality remain valid, so backward compatibility is preserved.

patterns.mechanism: The speculative goal of a unified grime/wear/buildup geometry layer is anchored by a concrete binding schema and performance-tuned LOD strategies.

math.wetness_integration: w_next = clamp01(w_prev + r * Δt - d * (1 - rainIntensity) * Δt)
math.mud_decay: m_next = clamp01(m_prev + m_rate * Δt - k * (rainIntensity * α + movementSpeed * β) * Δt)

missingPieces: Explicit `anchors` field in the research-topic schema linking speculative topics to concrete topicIds.
CI rule that rejects new speculative topics without at least one declared anchor.
Example notebooks or test scenes demonstrating end-to-end: speculative idea → concrete implementation → telemetry.

addField.name: anchors
addField.description: Optional list of topicIds that this topic is anchored to. Required for speculative topics to ensure a concrete implementation path.

sections.title: Internal and Industry Terminology Integration
sections.summary: How internal GameNext terms and external engine concepts form a unified, schema-backed dictionary.

internalTerms.definition: A scalar in [0,1] describing how strongly grime adheres to a surface before being removed by environmental effects or motion.
Constellation Integrity Coefficient; a scalar in [0,1] that combines signal confidence, model stability, and internal consistency checks.
Latency Safety Guard; a scalar in [0,1] that modulates the rate of change of visual and audio parameters to avoid sudden, disorienting shifts.
A scalar in [0,1] representing the normalized radius of a visual mask or vignette from the screen center.

industryTerms.definition: An approach to virtualized geometry where meshes are stored and streamed at very high detail using hierarchical or cluster-based LOD, enabling billions of triangles in a scene.
integrationNotes: GameNext bindings should treat nanite-like geometry as a target for density-aware effects, e.g., grime density scaled by triangle density and screen-space size.
BCI-driven parameters (e.g., motionSmear) must respect existing LOD transitions to avoid popping artifacts.

industryTerms.definition: Logical routing channels in an audio engine that carry spatialized audio streams, which can be mixed, filtered, and controlled independently.
integrationNotes: Attributes like infectedChannelGain, squadMuffle, heartbeatGain, breathGain, and ringingLevel should map onto specific bus gains and filters.
Direct is typically mapped to a non-spatial or minimally processed UI/alert bus.

missingPieces: A formal `engine_id` registry that ties industry terms to concrete engine subsystems.
Automated checks to ensure internal terms used in schemas and Rust structs have definition entries.
End-to-end examples where a term definition, a schema field, and an engine mapping are all visible in one doc.

proposedTools.description: Scans schemas and Rust crates, ensures all internal terms are present in ai-definition-response entries, and flags mismatches.
Builds a human-readable glossary from the term registry for inclusion in docs.

sections.title: Engine Compatibility Index and Gap Analysis
sections.summary: Formalizes how compatibility with engines is modeled, tracked, and used to drive new research topics.

schema: CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS engine_registry (
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS engine_contract_compat (
engine_id     TEXT NOT NULL REFERENCES engine_registry(engine_id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
contract_id   TEXT NOT NULL REFERENCES contract_registry(contract_id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
support_level TEXT NOT NULL CHECK (support_level IN ('native','adapter','planned','unsupported')),
CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_engine_compat_support

supportLevelGuidelines.definition: The contract can be wired into the engine using documented, stable APIs with no custom logic beyond simple parameter assignment.
supportLevelGuidelines.example: A GameNext grime binding mapped directly into an engine's material parameter collection with no additional shader code.

supportLevelGuidelines.definition: The contract can be integrated via a thin, reusable glue layer that translates GameNext data into engine-specific constructs.
supportLevelGuidelines.example: A Rust crate plus small engine plugin that converts BCI geometry bindings into engine material and audio parameters.

supportLevelGuidelines.definition: Integration is desired and tracked but not implemented; may have design docs or issues.
supportLevelGuidelines.example: A research topic exists for mapping new BCI fields to a particular engine’s post-processing stack.

supportLevelGuidelines.definition: No known integration; either technically infeasible or not yet investigated.
supportLevelGuidelines.example: An advanced BCI binding that depends on engine features not yet available.

proposedFunctions.description: Inserts or updates a row in engine_contract_compat to record compatibility.
Returns contracts that are missing from engine_contract_compat or marked as unsupported.

missingPieces: Rust utility functions that wrap the SQL engine_compat schema.
Linking gap analysis results to automatic creation of new research topics in the research index.
Example reports or views that summarize coverage per engine and per domain.

sections.title: AI-First Integration and Tooling Pathways
sections.summary: Describes how AI tools interact with the research, definition, and compatibility layers to produce high-quality code and docs.

steps: AI queries research_topic + engine_contract_compat to find open topics relevant to a target engine and domain.
AI reads relatedSchemas and term definitions to understand the contract surfaces involved.
AI proposes new Rust modules, JSON schemas, or SQL extensions that align with existing contracts and invariants.
AI emits fully-formed files (no placeholders) and links them back to topicIds via meta fields for traceability.
AI calls find_gaps_for_engine(engineId) to identify unsupported contracts.
For each gap, AI creates or updates a research_topic entry with domain, hypothesis, and experiments.
AI can generate skeleton integration docs (not code stubs) that show how mappings might work in principle.

missingPieces: Standardized meta fields linking code files and topics (e.g., `meta.topicIds` in schemas or Rust modules).
Lightweight CLI helpers for querying and updating the research and compatibility indices.
Guidelines for model evaluation: how to check that AI-generated code respects safety and authenticity constraints.
